title_hint: Structuring a Claude Code Repository for Maximum AI Leverage
description: A practical guide to organising a Claude Code project so you get the most out of its built-in capabilities: custom skills (/design, /implement, /simplify), parallel subagents, CLAUDE.md context files, rules/, and the design-then-implement workflow. Covers folder layout, what to put in CLAUDE.md vs rules/ vs docs/, how to write good skill prompts, and when to decompose work into parallel subagents.
audience: Software engineers who are new to Claude Code and want to go beyond basic usage — they already know how to run the CLI but haven't yet structured their repo to take full advantage of it.
tone: practical, opinionated, tutorial-style with concrete examples
outline: Why repo structure matters for AI-assisted development — context windows, discoverability, prompt quality
CLAUDE.md: the single most important file — what to include (architecture, commands, conventions), what to leave out, the 200-line limit rule
rules/ directory: separating stable conventions (code-style, testing, git) from the main CLAUDE.md to keep it concise
docs/ directory: architecture.md, workflows.md, and specs/ for design artefacts
Custom skills: /design, /implement, /simplify — when to trigger each, how to write skill prompts that use Explore and Plan subagents effectively
Parallel subagents: the design-then-implement pattern, how to decompose a spec into independent tasks, avoiding context bleed between agents
The full workflow end-to-end: branch → /design → review spec → /implement → /simplify → test → commit
Common mistakes: putting too much in CLAUDE.md, skipping /design for non-trivial features, not isolating tests

title_hint: The Forbidden Model: Building a Company When Your AI Can Be Unplugged
description: A sarcastic, opinionated take on the US government restricting Anthropic's most powerful frontier model and what that means for everyone who built their entire stack on top of it. Explores the uncomfortable reality that modern software engineering — from code generation to architecture decisions — now depends on API keys that can be revoked by executive order. Contrasts the 'just use Claude/GPT' default with self-hosted alternatives like Ollama, llama.cpp, Mistral, and other models you can actually own. Offers concrete advice on building a modern tech company that doesn't collapse when a single provider goes dark, including local model deployment, model-agnostic abstractions, hybrid architectures, and the business case for AI sovereignty. The tone should be dripping with sarcasm — the kind of post someone writes after realising their entire CI pipeline depends on a model that a government official can ban over lunch.
audience: CTOs, tech leads, and senior engineers who have gone all-in on cloud AI APIs and are now nervously reading geopolitics news. Also founders building AI-native companies who want to sleep at night.
tone: sarcastic, dark-humored, opinionated — think 'I told you so' energy mixed with genuine practical advice. Deploy war story vibes. The sarcasm should make the serious points hit harder, not soften them.
outline: The punchline: you built your company on someone else's brain and now the government is debating whether to lobotomize it — congratulations on your architecture choices
What actually happened: the US government restricting the most powerful Anthropic frontier model, why it matters beyond the headlines, and why 'it won't happen to my model' is copium

The dependency horror show: how modern software engineering quietly became addicted to frontier AI — code generation, PR reviews, architecture planning, testing, documentation — and what your bus factor looks like when the API returns 403

The Ollama manifesto: self-hosted models (Ollama, llama.cpp, vLLM, Mistral, Llama, Qwen, DeepSeek) you can run on your own hardware, what they can and can't do today, and why 'but it's not as good as Claude' is missing the point

Building a company without a single point of AI failure: model-agnostic abstraction layers, hybrid local+cloud architectures, fallback chains, keeping your prompts portable, and why your LLM should be a swappable dependency not a load-bearing wall
The procurement checklist nobody wants: evaluating AI providers like you'd evaluate any critical vendor — exit strategy, data residency, geopolitical risk, what happens when the ToS changes at 2am

Concrete mitigation strategies: run critical paths on local models, keep cloud AI for nice-to-haves, version your prompts like code, test against multiple backends, build kill switches, maintain a 'no-AI' degraded mode
The uncomfortable truth: the companies that survive the next AI regulation wave won't be the ones with the best models — they'll be the ones that treated AI as a tool, not a foundation
